data.title: 5 Takeaways from Illinois Senate Democratic debate for Durbin's seat
data.description:
Illinois Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton (D) and Reps. Robin Kelly (D-Ill.) and Raja Krishnamoorthi (D-Ill.) went toe-to-toe in an hour-long debate on Monday night as the three vie for the Democratic nod to succeed retiring Senate Minority Whip Dick Durbin (D-Ill.) in March.

Stratton, Kelly and Krishnamoorthi participated in a debate hosted by The Chicago Sun-Times, WBEZ, and the University of Chicago Institute of Politics ahead of their March 17 contest.

While President Trump and his administration loomed large over the debate, particularly the two U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE)-involved shootings in Minnesota that rocked the country, Stratton, in particular, also took repeated swipes at Kristhnamoorthi, the frontrunner in the race.

Here are five takeaways from the Illinois Senate Democratic debate:

ICE becomes early focus

The ICE-involved shootings of 37-year-old Alex Pretti and 37-year-old Renee Good became an early focus on the debate when one of the moderators first asked the candidates what legislative reforms they would seek from the Department of Homeland Security if elected to the Senate and how they would work toward getting them done with a Republican-controlled federal government in light of the two shootings.

All three denounced the Trump administration and all backed calls to abolish the agency. During the debate, Kelly touted her role as the lead sponsor of legislation to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem.

“Right now, I’m challenging the political machine,” Kelly said. “I have filed letters of impeachment against Kristi Noem, and I’m leading that fight.”

Stratton, however, used the topic of ICE to attack Krishnamoorthi, pointing out that he voted in favor of a resolution thanking the agency. The focus of the resolution, passed in the House last year, was on condemning an antisemitic attack in Boulder, Colo.

Part of the resolution included language saying that the House “expresses gratitude to law enforcement officers, including U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement personnel, for protecting the homeland.”

“Ninety-nine percent of that resolution was about condemning antisemitism following an attack on 14 Jews in Boulder, Colo,” Kristhnamoorthi said at one point, defending his vote.

“I’m going to condemn antisemitism eight days out of the week, and that was one that the Jewish community especially wanted me to vote for,” he added.

Stratton goes on attack against Krishnamoorthi

The lieutenant governor repeatedly attacked Krishnamoorthi during the debate, underscoring his frontrunner status in the race. The congressman has led in virtually every public poll on the Democratic contest and enjoys an impressive cash advantage.

Krishnamoorthi is starting the year with over $15 million cash on hand and brought in close to $3.6 million between October and December. Kelly and Stratton have not announced their fourth-quarter sums or cash on hand, but candidates have until the end of the month to report their last fundraising numbers. Both Kelly and Stratton have trailed Krishnamoorthi in cash advantage.

At one point during the debate on a question about supporting term limits, Stratton suggested Krishnamoorthi had been an ineffective member of Congress, saying he had only gotten four bills passed and signed into law. She noted they were all specifically about naming certain postal offices.

“That’s not the kind of fight that people are looking for,” Stratton said of the congressman.

While Krishnamoorthi has had four pieces of legislation he sponsored signed into law, those numbers don’t take into account co-sponsoring legislation. Krishnamoorthi has had almost 80 bills he sponsored or co-sponsored signed into law.

“I didn’t just pass four bills. I actually passed the law that actually modernizes our nation’s skills-based education system, providing higher quality skills-based education for 11 million people annually,” Krishnamoorthi shot back. “I passed the law that provides mental health resources to healthcare professionals for the first time.”

Krishnamoorthi also hit Stratton on her track record of receiving corporate PAC money, noting she received corporate PAC donations when she was running for lieutenant governor and that an aligned political group of hers was also taking corporate PAC money.

“Juliana made a pledge not to accept any corporate PAC money in her Senate campaign, and she has upheld that pledge,” Stratton campaign spokeswoman Allison Janowski told The Chicago Sun-Times in November when asked about some of her past contributions.

Kelly largely stays out of the fray

While Stratton and Krishnamoorthi traded barbs during the debate, Kelly largely stayed out of the fray for the most part.

Public polling has largely shown Kelly trailing in third place, putting pressure on the congresswoman to have a standout moment during the debate. Though Kelly did lean into the fact that she was leading House Democrats’ impeachment into Noem, Stratton’s attacks on Krishnamoorthi took up much of the oxygen during the debate.

Kelly did, however, attack Stratton over her claims that she did not take corporate PAC money.

“The corporate PAC money I take, you see who I take corporate PAC money from, unlike the commercial that the lieutenant governor has. That is paid for by dark money,” Kelly said. The group Kelly was referring to is a super PAC, which is legally required to report its donors.

Trump looms large

There was no shortage of criticism and attacks geared toward the president and his administration during the debate, showing how Trump has continued to loom large in both Republican and Democratic primaries.

“Look, I’m running to try to preserve, protect, and defend the American Dream from mortal attack by Donald J. Trump,” Krishnamoorthi said during his closing remarks.

“We have to dismantle ICE, and we have to rebuild our system, but all the way around, not just ICE,” Kelly said when speaking about reforms to the Homeland Security Department, adding that they needed “to stop Kristi Noem’s reign of terror through all of our cities.”

Candidates cool to backing Schumer as Senate Dem leader

The candidates were largely cool to the idea of backing Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) as the Democratic leader in the upper chamber after 2026, underscoring how the issue has become a litmus test among Democratic candidates while also making Schumer politically toxic for them.

While Kelly and Krishnamoorthi left the door open to potentially backing him, Stratton was the only candidate who outright refused to back the New York Democrat as leader again.

“I’ve already said that I will not support Chuck Schumer as leader in the Senate, and I’m the only person on this stage that has said so,” Stratton said.

data.title: Warren calls for Kristi Noem to resign or face impeachment
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) said Congress should move to impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem if she does not resign from office.

In a video posted Monday to the social platform X, the Democratic senator joined growing calls for Noem’s impeachment amid growing concern over her handling of the administration’s crackdown on immigration in Minnesota and across the country.

“Kristi Noem should resign, and if she doesn’t, Congress should impeach her and remove her from office. In America, we still believe in accountability, not lies,” Warren said in the video.

The senator accused the secretary of lying to the American people about details of the fatal shooting by a Border Patrol agent of 37-year-old Alex Pretti, a Minneapolis resident and intensive care nurse at the city’s Veterans Affairs hospital, during a demonstration protesting the government’s immigration enforcement efforts.

Shortly after the incident, DHS officials were quick to accuse Pretti of wanting to “massacre law enforcement” and “murder federal agents.”

Video footage from bystanders, however, appeared to dispute that version of events. Pretti, who was licensed to carry a concealed weapon, was not seen holding his weapon at any point in the interaction in available footage. Instead, an agent appeared to take a concealed weapon from Pretti’s waistband moments before another agent shot him in the back.

“Donald Trump and Kristi Noem think that they can tell you what to believe. They think you will believe them instead of your own eyes. Don’t roll over for a lie. These lies have to stop,” Warren said. “We cannot allow this shooting and ICE’s blatant violations of law to be covered up. It’s time to rein in the federal agents who think they can swagger through our streets, throw people to the ground, shoot American citizens, and then count on protection from Trump and Noem.”

“We need a full, independent investigation and all wrongdoing held to account to the fullest extent of the law, the victims of this violence deserve justice,” she continued.

In response to growing calls for Noem’s impeachment, DHS assistant secretary Tricia Laughlin said in a statement to The Hill, “DHS enforces the laws Congress passes, period. If certain members don’t like those laws, changing them is literally their job.”

“While ICE officers are facing a staggering 1,300% spike in assaults, too many politicians would rather defend criminals and attack the men and women who are enforcing our laws and did nothing while Joe Biden facilitated an invasion of tens of millions of illegal aliens into our country. It’s time they focus on protecting the American people, the work this Department is doing every day under Secretary Noem’s leadership,” the statement continued.

data.title: Cruz advises Trump officials to tone down rhetoric on Minnesota shootings
data.description:
Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) urged the Trump administration to embrace a more measured tone in response to the officer-involved shootings in Minnesota, saying officials undermine their credibility by escalating their rhetoric before an investigation has taken place.

In the latest episode of his “Verdict” podcast, Cruz praised the Trump administration’s work to lower crime and secure the country’s border but expressed concern about its handling of the fatal shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti, two 37-year-old Minneapolis residents who died at anti-immigration demonstrations after they were shot by federal officers.

“What I think the administration could do better is the tone with which they’re describing this,” Cruz said. “That immediately when an incident like this happens, they come out guns blazing, that we took out a violent terrorist, hurray.”

“And the problem is, particularly for someone not paying attention, if you’re being told this is a mom of three, and there’s no indication [that she’s a violent criminal] — You know, she’s not waving an ISIS flag or doesn’t have a suicide vest around her — escalating the rhetoric doesn’t help. And it actually loses credibility,” Cruz continued.

“So I would encourage the administration to be more measured, to recognize the tragedy and, and to say, ‘We don’t want anyone’s lives to be lost. And the politicians who are pouring gasoline onto this fire, they need to stop,’” Cruz added.

After a Border Patrol agent shot and killed Pretti, an intensive care nurse at the city’s VA hospital, on Saturday morning, DHS officials were quick to accuse him of wanting to “massacre law enforcement” and “murder federal agents.”

Video footage from bystanders circulating on social media, however, appeared to dispute that version of events. Pretti, who was licensed to carry a concealed weapon, was not seen holding his weapon at any point in the interaction. Instead, an agent appeared to take a concealed weapon from Pretti’s waistband moments before another agent shot him in the back.

The incident has compelled even some Republicans to publicly express concern about the incident, as many called for thorough investigations into the incident.

Cruz, similarly, said he would wait for an investigation before drawing any conclusion about the agent’s actions.

“Everyone who has anything resembling a conscience is horrified by what’s happening in Minneapolis. And what I want to call on everyone to do is just ratchet things down, ratchet the rhetoric down, ratchet the anger down. You know, we are a nation and we’re supposed to be one nation,” he said, noting, right now, there appears to be a “deliberate confrontation” between two sides.

“On one side, they view every shooting as an absolute tragedy, as evidence that America has become Nazi Germany,” Cruz said, slamming Democratic leaders for comparing federal immigration enforcement to the Gestapo.

“On the other side, you see some people that seem pretty eager to celebrate a violent and lethal confrontation with law enforcement,” he added.

The Texas senator called both fatal shootings in Minneapolis “a tragedy.”

“Anytime you have an American who is killed in a confrontation with law enforcement, it is tragic that a life is lost. Particularly in this instance. We now have two people, two Americans, who’ve been killed in confrontations with law enforcement, who, from all appearances, are not violent criminals.”

data.title: Democrat Suozzi says he 'failed' with vote to pass DHS funding bill: 'I must do a better job'
data.description:
Rep. Tom Suozzi (D-N.Y.) expressed regret on Monday for voting last week to fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

In a statement on Facebook, the moderate Democrat said the backlash from his constituents — paired with the fatal shooting of 37-year-old Minneapolis resident Alex Pretti by a Border Patrol officer — has compelled him to reconsider his vote.

“I failed to view the DHS funding vote as a referendum on the illegal and immoral conduct of ICE in Minneapolis,” Suozzi said in his post.

“I hear the anger from my constituents, and I take responsibility for that,” he continued. “I have long been critical of ICE’s unlawful behavior and I must do a better job demonstrating that.”

The congressman called on the Trump administration to end its immigration crackdown in Minnesota, pointing to the “chaos” and “tragedy” that he said stems from the federal officers’ presence there.

“The senseless and tragic murder of Alex Pretti underscores what happens when untrained federal agents operate without accountability,” Suozzi said. “President Trump must immediately end ‘Operation Metro Surge’ and ICE’s occupation of Minneapolis that has sown chaos, led to tragedy, and undermined experienced local law enforcement.”

Suozzi was one of seven House Democrats on Thursday to join all but one House Republican in approving a $64.4 billion bill funding DHS, including roughly $10 billion for ICE. The bill also provides billions of dollars in funding for the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), the Transportation Security Administration (TSA), the U.S. Secret Service, the Disaster Relief Fund and other programs and agencies. 

Suozzi explained his vote earlier Thursday, writing that the measure was the product of “bipartisan negotiations and responsible governing,” framing the vote as necessary to avert a potential government shutdown. The deadline to pass the remaining appropriations bills for fiscal 2026 is Jan. 30.

“I am voting for the Department of Homeland Security appropriations bill, not to expand ICE enforcement or add more agents, but to fund the core operations Americans rely on every day, FEMA disaster response, TSA security, Customs and Border Protection, the Coast Guard, passport processing, and other essential services,” Suozzi wrote on the social platform X at the time.

“I strongly oppose allowing another shutdown to disrupt the lives of working families. We saw the damage of a shutdown last year: millions of Americans lost pay and access to essential services,” he added. “Congress has a responsibility to govern, not to lurch from one manufactured crisis to the next.”

data.title: Google settles for $68 million after lawsuit claimed it secretly recorded users
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Google has agreed to pay $68 million to settle a class action lawsuit accusing it of secretly recording smart device users, according to a court document filed Friday in Northern California.

The case centers around the Google Assistant tool, which is only supposed to record when so-called hot word phrases such as, “Hey Google,” or “Okay Google” are used, or when someone manually presses a button on the device.

The lawsuit alleges that Google inappropriately started recording personal conversations when no hot word was used, unbeknownst to owners of Google smartphones, home speakers, laptops, tablets, Chromecast media players and even wireless earphones.

Users complained they were targeted with ads based on things they had said, even when they hadn’t activated their smart devices using a hot word, according to Reuters.

Google, which didn’t acknowledge any fault, said it decided to settle in order to avoid the “uncertainty, risk, expense, inconvenience and distraction” of a lengthy court battle, according to court documents.

The preliminary filing in San Jose, California federal court will now need approval by U.S. District Judge Beth Labson Freeman.

It wasn’t immediately clear how much individual plaintiffs would receive after attorney fees and other costs.

Apple agreed to settle a similar lawsuit regarding its assistant Siri for $95 million.

data.title: Appeals court declines to reimpose restrictions on agents at Minnesota protests
data.description:
A federal appeals court on Monday declined to reimpose restrictions on federal agents at protests in Minnesota, refusing the American Civil Liberties Union’s (ACLU) plea to do so following this weekend’s fatal shooting. 

The 8th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals panel sided with the Trump administration, ruling that limits imposed by a judge earlier this month were too broad and vague. 

“A wrong call could end in contempt, yet there is little in the order that constrains the district court’s power to impose it,” the panel wrote in its unsigned opinion. 

The ruling lasts until the 8th Circuit resolves the administration’s appeal in normal course, which will be expedited. 

U.S. District Judge Katherine Menendez, an appointee of former President Biden, had blocked federal personnel responding to protests in Minnesota from retaliating against peaceful demonstrators or using pepper spray and “similar nonlethal munitions and crowd dispersal tools” against them. 

She issued the ruling after a group of residents sued on Dec. 17, claiming that officers were violating the First Amendment at protests that erupted across the Twin Cities as federal resources arrived. 

The Trump administration argued the judge had no authority to impose the restrictions, further saying they endangered immigration officers and public safety. 

Saturday’s fatal shooting of 37-year-old Alex Pretti by a U.S. Customs and Border Protection agent added a new layer to the legal battle. The shooting occurred after the administration already commenced an emergency appeal. 

The 8th Circuit by that point had agreed to briefly put the judge’s restrictions on hold until the next stage of the litigation. But after the shooting, the ACLU went back to court over the weekend, urging the 8th Circuit to restore the restrictions in “light of escalating, imminent risks.” 

Monday’s ruling refusing to do so makes no mention of the shooting.  

The three judges were all appointed by Republican presidents. 

U.S. Circuit Judges Raymond Gruender and Bobby Shepherd were appointed by former President George W. Bush. U.S. Circuit Judge David Stras was appointed by President Trump. 

Gruender wrote separately that he would’ve reimposed the judge’s restriction preventing federal agents from using pepper spray on peaceful demonstrators, saying that part was specific enough. 

“That directive is not an improperly vague ‘obey the law’ injunction and should not be stayed pending appeal,” the judge wrote.

data.title: Noem agrees to testify before Senate Judiciary in March
data.description:
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem agreed to testify before the Senate Judiciary Committee in early March, a spokesperson for the committee’s chair, Sen. Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa), confirmed to The Hill on Monday.

Noem is expected to testify on March 3, according to Grassley’s office, for one round of questioning, and senators will each be allotted 10 minutes for questions.

The confirmation comes amid fresh scrutiny of Noem’s handling of her post after federal officials fatally shot Renee Good and Alex Pretti, both 37-year-old Minneapolis residents, during demonstrations against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations just this month.

The top Democrat on the Senate Judiciary Committee, ranking member Dick Durbin (Ill.), nodded to the growing calls for Noem’s impeachment, in a statement responding to Grassley’s announcement of her upcoming testimony.

“Secretary Noem refused to appear before the Senate Judiciary Committee last year and now tells us that she will be available in five weeks—should she still be DHS [Department of Homeland Security] Secretary at that time,” Durbin said in a statement.

“With all of the violence and deaths involving DHS, the Secretary is apparently in no hurry to account for her mismanagement of this national crisis. And she expects us to rubber stamp her record-breaking budget in the meantime,” he continued.

The testimony is not tethered to the latest backlash Noem is facing, but she undoubtedly will face questions from senators seeking information about the fatal shootings and the aggressive immigration enforcement operations taking place in Minneapolis and around the country.

data.title: Trump increases South Korea tariffs, slamming legislature for not approving US deal
data.description:
President Trump on Monday said he was increasing tariffs on South Korea while slamming the country’s Legislature for not living up to a $350 billion deal. 

“South Korea’s Legislature is not living up to its Deal with the United States. President Lee and I reached a Great Deal for both Countries on July 30, 2025, and we reaffirmed these terms while I was in Korea on October 29, 2025. Why hasn’t the Korean Legislature approved it?” the president wrote in a post on Truth Social.

“Because the Korean Legislature hasn’t enacted our Historic Trade Agreement, which is their prerogative, I am hereby increasing South Korean TARIFFS on Autos, Lumber, Pharma, and all other Reciprocal TARIFFS, from 15% to 25%. Thank you for your attention to this matter!” he added.

Last October, Trump said leaders in Seoul agreed to purchase oil and gas from the United States in “vast quantities” while their companies agreed to invest $600 billion in exchange for lower tariffs.

data.title: The Memo: White House pulls back amid public uproar over Alex Pretti killing
data.description:
The Trump White House on Monday made a rare rhetorical retreat amid public uproar over its initial response to the killing of Alex Pretti, the second U.S. citizen recently shot dead by federal law enforcement agents working in Minneapolis as part of the administration’s deportation efforts.

A nation that was already simmering after Renee Good’s death at the hands of an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent on Jan. 7 erupted on Saturday when Pretti, a 37-year-old ICU nurse, was also shot and killed, this time by a Border Patrol agent.

The first flames of public anger erupted when his harrowing death was widely seen on social media. They were fanned into a near-inferno by comments from administration officials blaming him for what had occurred.

Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said Pretti “committed an act of domestic terrorism,” key White House aide Stephen Miller called him a “would-be assassin” and senior Border Patrol official Greg Bovino said he wanted to “massacre law enforcement.”

Those claims are not supported by the available evidence.

Though Pretti was in possession of a gun, the firearm was legally held, and the videos released so far never show him with it in his hand. The gun appears to have been taken away before he was shot. Video analyses have found agents fired at least 10 shots.

Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) was among the voices calling the killing an “execution.”

A statement from Pretti’s family expressed revulsion at “the sickening lies told about our son by the administration” and referred to “Trump’s murdering and cowardly ICE thugs.”

On Monday, Trump himself led a softening of tone. The shift is a de facto recognition of the depth of public anger, even as critics will say there is a need for a more substantive reckoning.

First, in what was widely seen as an implied rebuke to the combative Bovino, Trump wrote on social media that he would send border czar Tom Homan to Minnesota “tonight.” The president characterized Homan as “tough but fair” and conspicuously added that he would “report directly to me.”

Later in the day, multiple outlets reported that Bovino and some of the Border Patrol agents working with him were expected to leave Minneapolis soon, perhaps as early as Tuesday.

Roughly three hours after the Homan announcement, Trump posted again to social media about a call with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D). Trump has previously excoriated Walz, while for his part the governor has accused federal agents in his state of “a campaign of organized brutality.”

This time, however, Trump said, “It was a very good call and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength.” Trump also claimed that Walz was “happy” about the Homan announcement.

Walz called the conversation with Trump “productive” in a statement which also underscored the governor’s desire for “impartial investigations” into the killings of Good and Pretti.

Later in the day, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt continued the shift, tellingly distancing Trump from the language used by aides.

One reporter read Leavitt some of the descriptions of Pretti by Noem and Miller and asked, “Does the president agree with them?”

“Look, as I’ve said, I have not heard the president characterize Mr. Pretti in that way,” the press secretary responded. “However, I have heard the president say he wants to let the facts and the investigation lead itself.”

She sounded a similar tone in response to several questions about the officials’ earlier comments.

In aggregate, the White House approach on Monday revealed the political reality. 

At a time when reaction to almost any public event, no matter how traumatic, breaks along predictable, ideologically tribal lines, the killing of Pretti has been an exception.

The initial defiance from figures like Noem and Miller provoked unusually direct criticism from conservative quarters. On Capitol Hill, even some staunch Trump allies went public with their unease.

A Monday editorial in The Wall Street Journal, whose op-ed pages are reliably conservative, declared that ICE’s operations in Minneapolis were “becoming a moral and political debacle for the Trump Presidency.”

A New York Post editorial concluded by telling Trump, “the American people didn’t vote for these scenes, and you can’t continue to order them to not believe their lying eyes.” 

On Sunday afternoon, Sen. Pete Ricketts (R-Neb.) said the nation had “witnessed a horrifying situation” in Minneapolis and, while reiterating his support for ICE, added, “I expect a prioritized, transparent investigation into this incident.”

Those kinds of reactions seemed to be picking up momentum on Monday. Sen. John Curtis (R-Utah) complained about Noem’s “premature” response to Pretti’s killing, which he said “weakened confidence.”

Texas Gov. Greg Abbott (R) told a radio show in his state that the White House needs to “recalibrate on what needs to be done.”

There are, to be sure, many parts of the political picture that remain unsettled.

It’s unknowable how long Trump — synonymous with strident rhetoric on immigration — will stick with his more conciliatory tone. Leavitt, for all the distance she sought to create between Trump and the Noem-Miller-Bovino rhetoric, also blamed local law enforcement and Democratic officials for not cooperating more readily with immigration enforcement efforts. On the Democratic side, there are tensions over how far to go in condemnation of ICE.

There is also the most macabre unknowable of all: what happens if there is another fatality committed by, or against, federal agents?

But alongside the terrible human cost, it’s become clear that the killings of Good and Pretti have produced a rare political moment.

It’s one where ideologically disparate voices said “stop” to Trump — and he might, perhaps, heed them.

The Memo is a reported column by Niall Stanage.

data.title: Senate Democrat: 'Noem's got to go'
data.description:
Sen. Peter Welch (D-Vt.) called for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to be removed as the leader of the government agency that oversees immigration enforcement operations, saying he would support her impeachment if she doesn’t resign.

In an interview Monday on MS NOW’s “Ana Cabrera Reports,” the Democratic senator called Noem “the real problem” as he criticized her handling of the fatal federal officer-involved shootings this month of Renee Good and Alex Pretti, both 37-year-old Minneapolis residents who died while protesting immigration operations in their city.

“Noem’s got to go. I mean, we have the head of this department who spends a lot of her time in self-promotion, the $200 million video shoot in Mount Rushmore,” Welch said in the interview. “And then when there is a serious, serious situation involving the death of two [Minnesota] citizens, she calls them domestic terrorists.”

“No one has confidence that she’s doing this job on the level,” the senator added.

Host Ana Cabrera noted that Noem was unlikely to resign and asked whether Welch would support impeaching the secretary.

“I would, if that’s what it took,” Welch said, “but she should resign.”

Welch said Noem has “blown the job” of Homeland Security secretary, characterizing the immigration crackdown as “mass intimidation,” not effective law enforcement.  

“She is the real problem because what she’s doing is turning the function of immigration enforcement away from enforcement and into being kind of a shock troop that goes into cities and acts with impunity,” Welch said. “She’s blown the job. You know, we need immigration enforcement, but that’s not what she’s doing. It’s mass intimidation.”

Welch is the latest Democrat to respond to the fatal shootings in Minneapolis by calling for Noem’s impeachment.

The Democratic Party’s official account on the social platform X backed the push for Noem’s impeachment, writing in a post Sunday, “IMPEACH KRISTI NOEM.”

The Hill has reached out to DHS for a response to Welch’s comments.

In response to other calls for Noem’s impeachment, Homeland Security Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin said, “DHS enforces the laws Congress passes, period.”

“If certain members don’t like those laws, changing them is literally their job,” she added, in the statement.
